assets.title: Bloodroot Red
assets.description: Symbolizes strength, victory, and the earth. Traditional dye made from bloodroot.

assets.title: Yellowroot Gold
assets.description: Symbolizes intelligence and warmth. Natural dye derived from yellowroot plants.

assets.title: Walnut Black
assets.description: Symbolizes the West and depth. Derived from the hulls of black walnuts.

assets.title: Clay Terracotta
assets.description: The natural shade of coiled clay used in traditional Cherokee pottery.

assets.title: Slate Blue
assets.description: Associated with the North and protection, mimicking skies and stone.

assets.title: Rivercane Double-Weave
assets.description: Traditional rivercane double-weave basket. One basket woven inside another, creating strength and beautiful symmetry.

assets.title: Rivercane Plaiting Texture
assets.description: A close-up of rivercane splints showing the technical precision of traditional diagonal plaiting designs.

assets.title: Traditional Clay Pottery
assets.description: Hand-coiled earthenware showing textured stamp marks made by hand-carved wooden paddles before firing.

assets.title: Curvilinear Seed Beadwork
assets.description: Detailed beadwork using vibrant red, orange, white, and sky blue trade beads in traditional fire/flow paths.

assets.title: Cherokee Nation
assets.content:
### Cherokee Nation

*   **Official Website:** [cherokee.org](https://www.cherokee.org)
*   **Location:** Tahlequah, Oklahoma
*   **Tribal Seal:** Incorporates a seven-pointed star encircled by oak leaves, symbolizing the seven clans and the eternal sacred fire.

*A major center of cultural preservation, language revitalization, and support for contemporary artists via the Cherokee Artist Recovery Act (ARA).*

assets.title: Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians
assets.content:
### Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians

*   **Official Website:** [ebci.gov](https://ebci.gov)
*   **Location:** Qualla Boundary, North Carolina
*   **Cultural Hub:** Home to the Museum of the Cherokee People and the Qualla Arts and Crafts Mutual cooperative.

*Qualla Arts (founded 1946) is the oldest Native marketing cooperative in the US, preserving techniques of double-weaving, oak carving, and natural dyeing.*

assets.title: United Keetoowah Band (UKB)
assets.content:
### United Keetoowah Band

*   **Official Website:** [ukb-nsn.gov](https://ukb-nsn.gov)
*   **Location:** Tahlequah, Oklahoma
*   **Heritage Focus:** Maintains traditional Giduwa Cherokee customs, dialect, and historic clan identities.

*Supports local artisans through the John Hair Cultural Center and Museum, providing vital spaces for traditional regalia makers, storytellers, and weavers.*

assets.title: Linguistic Heritage
assets.content:
### Sequoyah's Syllabary

## ᏣᎳᎩ

**Tsalagi** (Cherokee)

In 1821, Sequoyah completed his revolutionary writing system of 85 characters. Today, the syllabary is not only a tool for literacy and preservation, but has evolved into a bold, high-contrast visual design element featured in contemporary digital graphics, typography, and streetwear.

assets.title: The Seven-Pointed Star
assets.description: Represents the seven clans of the Cherokee people: Wolf, Deer, Long Hair, Paint, Blue, Wild Potato, and Bird. It also stands as a symbol of unity, national sovereignty, and the flame of the sacred fire.

assets.title: Jeff Edwards (Modern Adaptations)
assets.content:
### Contemporary Graphic Art

**Jeff Edwards** is a celebrated contemporary Cherokee Nation graphic artist. He merges digital graphic design, vector pop art, and high-impact layouts with Sequoyah's Syllabary.

His works treat Cherokee characters not just as text, but as profound visual structures, demonstrating how ancestral language can live dynamically in digital mediums, print, and physical installations.

assets.title: Roy Boney Jr. (Visual Storytelling)
assets.content:
### Roy Boney Jr. — Language & Art

Roy Boney Jr. is an award-winning Cherokee artist, animator, and writer. He explores the intersection of comic book aesthetics, digital illustration, and traditional oral histories.

By incorporating Cherokee text and cultural themes directly into sequential graphic art, Boney challenges stereotypes and pioneers modern digital avenues for tribal storytelling and language preservation.
